questions.answer: A. L(G) = {a^m b | m >= 1} union {a^2 b^n | n >= 2}
questions.to_find: The language L(G) generated by the given context-free grammar G

understand.plain: We are given a context-free grammar with start variable S, non-terminals A and B, and terminals a and b. We need to trace all possible derivations from S to find exactly which strings the grammar generates. The two productions from S lead to two separate families of strings.

keywords.explain: A formal grammar where each production rule has exactly one non-terminal on the left-hand side. It defines a context-free language.
keywords.example: S -> aS | b generates {a^n b | n >= 0}

keywords.explain: The process of repeatedly applying grammar rules starting from the start symbol to produce a terminal string.
keywords.example: S -> aA -> aaA -> aaa shows three derivation steps

keywords.explain: The set of all terminal strings that can be derived from the start symbol of the grammar.
keywords.example: For S -> a | b, L(G) = {a, b}

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='240' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Grammar Derivation Tree</text><rect x='220' y='55' width='80' height='32' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='260' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>S</text><line x1='200' y1='87' x2='110' y2='115' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='320' y1='87' x2='410' y2='115' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='50' y='115' width='120' height='32' rx='6' class='bg' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='110' y='136' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>S -> aaBB (path 1)</text><rect x='350' y='115' width='120' height='32' rx='6' class='bg' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='410' y='136' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>S -> Ab (path 2)</text><line x1='110' y1='147' x2='110' y2='175' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='410' y1='147' x2='410' y2='175' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='40' y='175' width='140' height='32' rx='6' class='ok' rx='6'/><text x='110' y='196' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>a^2 b^n, n >= 2</text><rect x='340' y='175' width='140' height='32' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='410' y='196' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>a^m b, m >= 1</text><text x='260' y='240' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>B -> b^+ gives n>=2; A -> a^+ gives m>=1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Derivation tree showing S splits into two paths: S->aaBB producing a^2 b^n (n>=2), and S->Ab producing a^m b (m>=1)

given.aim: Find the complete language generated by grammar G with productions S->aaBB|Ab, A->a|aA, B->b|bB

terms.meaning: Non-terminal A generates one or more a's: a, aa, aaa, ... = a^m for m >= 1
terms.example: A => a, A => aA => aa, A => aA => aaA => aaa
terms.connects: When S -> Ab, A generates a^m, so Ab generates a^m b for m >= 1

terms.meaning: Non-terminal B generates one or more b's: b, bb, bbb, ... = b^n for n >= 1
terms.example: B => b, B => bB => bb, B => bB => bbB => bbb
terms.connects: When S -> aaBB, each B generates b^+ independently, so BB generates b^(n1+n2) for n1,n2 >= 1, meaning b^n for n >= 2

terms.meaning: Start symbol has two productions leading to two disjoint families of strings
terms.example: S -> aaBB -> aa bb = aabb; S -> Ab -> ab
terms.connects: The two families are {a^2 b^n | n >= 2} from aaBB and {a^m b | m >= 1} from Ab

given.plan: Trace each production from S separately. For S->aaBB: aa is fixed, B->b^+ gives at least one b per B, so BB gives b^n for n>=2, yielding a^2 b^n (n>=2). For S->Ab: A->a^+ gives a^m, so Ab yields a^m b (m>=1). The union of these two families is L(G).

steps.title: Analyze derivations from S -> Ab
steps.formula_raw: A ->* a^m for m >= 1, so Ab ->* a^m b
steps.apply: A -> a gives m=1: Ab => ab. A -> aA -> aaA -> ... -> a^m gives Ab => a^m b for any m >= 1. So this production generates {a^m b | m >= 1} = {ab, aab, aaab, ...}.
steps.note: The b at the end is exactly one b, always — it comes directly from the production S -> Ab.

steps.title: Analyze derivations from S -> aaBB
steps.formula_raw: B ->* b^n for n >= 1, so BB ->* b^(n1) b^(n2) = b^k for k >= 2
steps.apply: The prefix 'aa' is fixed. Each B independently generates b^+ (at least one b). So BB generates b^(n1+n2) where n1 >= 1 and n2 >= 1, meaning the total number of b's is at least 2. Thus aaBB generates {a^2 b^k | k >= 2} = {aabb, aabbb, aabbbb, ...}.
steps.note: The two a's are fixed from the literal 'aa' in the production — A is not involved here.

steps.title: Combine both families
steps.apply: L(S->Ab) = {a^m b | m >= 1} and L(S->aaBB) = {a^2 b^n | n >= 2}. Note: 'a^2 b' (i.e., aab) is in the first family (m=2) but not the second (second needs n>=2 b's). 'a^2 bb' = aabb is only in the second family. These two families overlap only if a string has exactly 2 a's followed by exactly 1 b — i.e., 'aab', which belongs to the first family. So L(G) = {a^m b | m >= 1} union {a^2 b^n | n >= 2}.

solution.result: L(G) = {a^m b | m >= 1} union {a^2 b^n | n >= 2}, which is option A.

questions.formula_note: Context-free grammar derivation tracing: separately analyze each production from the start symbol, determine the language each generates, then union the results.

questions.question:
Let G_1, G_2 be Context Free Grammars (CFGs) and R be a regular expression. For a grammar G, let L(G) denote the language generated by G. Which ONE among the following questions is decidable?

A. Is L(G_1) = L(G_2)?
B. Is L(G_1) ∩ L(G_2) = emptyset?
C. Is L(G_1) = L(R)?
D. Is L(G_1) = emptyset?
questions.to_find: Which of the given problems about CFGs is decidable

understand.plain: Decidability asks whether an algorithm (Turing machine) can always give a yes/no answer in finite time for any input. For CFLs, some problems are decidable (like emptiness testing) while others are undecidable (like equivalence). We need to identify which of the four given questions can always be answered algorithmically.

keywords.explain: A problem for which there exists an algorithm that always halts and gives the correct yes/no answer for every input.
keywords.example: Is a given DFA's language empty? Yes — just check if any accepting state is reachable.

keywords.explain: The problem of deciding whether a context-free grammar generates at least one string. This is decidable by marking productive non-terminals.
keywords.example: G: S -> aS (no base case) generates no strings, so L(G) = emptyset. An algorithm can detect this.

keywords.explain: The problem of deciding whether two CFGs generate the same language. This is undecidable — no algorithm can solve it for all CFGs.
keywords.example: Given G1 and G2, no algorithm can always determine if L(G1) = L(G2).

understand.visual_alt: Four problems about CFGs: equivalence of two CFGs (undecidable), intersection emptiness of two CFGs (undecidable), equivalence with a regular language (undecidable), and emptiness of a single CFG (decidable).

given.aim: Identify which decision problem about context-free grammars is decidable

terms.meaning: CFG equivalence — asking if two CFGs generate the same language
terms.example: Do G1: S->ab and G2: S->ab generate the same language? Yes, but no general algorithm exists.
terms.connects: This is undecidable — it reduces to the Post Correspondence Problem.

terms.meaning: CFL intersection emptiness — does the intersection of two CFLs contain any string?
terms.example: Intersection of two CFLs may not itself be a CFL, making this hard to decide.
terms.connects: This is undecidable — it can encode undecidable problems about CFGs.

terms.meaning: Asking if a CFL equals a regular language described by regex R
terms.example: Even though R is regular, determining if the CFL exactly equals it is undecidable.
terms.connects: Undecidable — CFL universality (Is L(G) = Sigma*?) is undecidable, and this is a special case.

terms.meaning: CFL emptiness — does the grammar generate any string at all?
terms.example: Mark all non-terminals that can derive terminal strings. If S is never marked, L(G) = emptyset.
terms.connects: This IS decidable — a simple marking algorithm determines if the start symbol is productive.

given.plan: Check each option against known decidability results for CFLs: emptiness is decidable; equivalence, intersection emptiness, and universality are all undecidable for CFGs.

steps.title: Check options A, B, C for decidability
steps.apply: Option A (L(G1) = L(G2)?): CFG equivalence is undecidable — proved by reduction from PCP. Option B (L(G1) ∩ L(G2) = emptyset?): CFL intersection emptiness is undecidable. Option C (L(G1) = L(R)?): This subsumes universality of CFLs (since L(R) could be Sigma*), which is undecidable.
steps.note: All three are classic undecidable problems for CFGs.

steps.title: Check option D: CFL Emptiness is decidable
steps.formula_raw: Mark non-terminal X as productive if X -> alpha where all symbols in alpha are already marked terminals or productive non-terminals
steps.apply: Algorithm: (1) Mark all terminals. (2) Repeatedly: if a production A -> w exists where all symbols in w are marked, mark A. (3) Repeat until no new marks. (4) If S is marked, L(G) != emptyset; otherwise L(G) = emptyset. This algorithm always terminates (finite grammar) and is correct. So 'Is L(G1) = emptyset?' is decidable.
steps.note: This is the standard 'productivity/reachability' algorithm for testing CFL emptiness — always terminates in polynomial time.

solution.result: Option D — 'Is L(G_1) = emptyset?' — is decidable. The other three options are undecidable problems for CFGs.

questions.formula_note: CFL emptiness is decidable via the productivity marking algorithm. CFL equivalence, intersection emptiness, and universality are all undecidable (reduce to Post Correspondence Problem or the halting problem).

questions.question:
State whether the following statement is TRUE or FALSE:

The intersection of two CFLs (context-free languages) is also a CFL.

A. TRUE
B. FALSE
questions.to_find: Whether the class of context-free languages is closed under intersection

understand.plain: Context-free languages (CFLs) are not closed under intersection. This means that if you take two CFLs L1 and L2, their intersection L1 ∩ L2 is not necessarily a CFL — it can be a non-context-free language. The classic counterexample shows a language that is the intersection of two CFLs but is not itself a CFL.

keywords.explain: A language class is closed under an operation if applying that operation to languages in the class always produces a language that is still in the class.
keywords.example: Regular languages are closed under union: union of two regular languages is always regular.

keywords.explain: The intersection of two context-free languages is not necessarily context-free — CFLs are NOT closed under intersection.
keywords.example: L1 = {a^n b^n c^m} and L2 = {a^m b^n c^n} are both CFLs, but L1 ∩ L2 = {a^n b^n c^n} is not a CFL.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='460' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>CFL Closure: Counterexample</text><rect x='20' y='55' width='200' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>L1 (CFL)</text><text x='120' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>{a^n b^n c^m | n,m >= 1}</text><rect x='260' y='55' width='200' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='360' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>L2 (CFL)</text><text x='360' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>{a^m b^n c^n | n,m >= 1}</text><text x='240' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>∩</text><rect x='130' y='155' width='220' height='45' rx='8' class='no'/><text x='240' y='174' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>L1 ∩ L2 = {a^n b^n c^n | n >= 1}</text><text x='240' y='192' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>NOT a CFL (pumping lemma proof)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing L1 = {a^n b^n c^m} and L2 = {a^m b^n c^n} are both CFLs, but their intersection {a^n b^n c^n} is not a CFL.

given.aim: Determine if CFLs are closed under intersection, i.e., whether L1 ∩ L2 is always a CFL when L1, L2 are CFLs

terms.meaning: Strings with equal a's and b's followed by any number of c's — this is a CFL
terms.connects: Generated by CFG: S -> AB, A -> aAb | ab, B -> cB | c

terms.meaning: Strings with any number of a's followed by equal b's and c's — this is a CFL
terms.connects: Generated by CFG: S -> AB, A -> aA | a, B -> bBc | bc

terms.meaning: Strings with equal numbers of a's, b's, and c's — this is the classic non-CFL
terms.example: abc, aabbcc, aaabbbccc are in L1 ∩ L2
terms.connects: By the pumping lemma for CFLs, {a^n b^n c^n} cannot be generated by any CFG

given.plan: Provide the classic counterexample: L1 = {a^n b^n c^m} and L2 = {a^m b^n c^n} are both CFLs, but their intersection is {a^n b^n c^n} which is provably not a CFL by the pumping lemma.

steps.title: Verify L1 and L2 are CFLs
steps.apply: L1 = {a^n b^n c^m | n,m >= 1}: A CFG is S -> AC, A -> aAb | ab, C -> cC | c. This is clearly context-free. L2 = {a^m b^n c^n | n,m >= 1}: A CFG is S -> AB, A -> aA | a, B -> bBc | bc. Also clearly context-free.
steps.note: Both languages require matching counts for two pairs of symbols but not all three, making them context-free.

steps.title: Compute L1 ∩ L2 and show it is not a CFL
steps.apply: A string is in L1 ∩ L2 iff it has equal a's and b's (from L1) AND equal b's and c's (from L2), which means equal a's, b's, and c's. So L1 ∩ L2 = {a^n b^n c^n | n >= 1}. By the CFL pumping lemma (with pumping length p, choose s = a^p b^p c^p, uvwxy must straddle at most two symbol types, so pumping changes relative counts), this language is NOT a CFL. Therefore the intersection of two CFLs need not be a CFL.
steps.note: {a^n b^n c^n} is a canonical example of a context-sensitive language that is not context-free. This proves CFLs are NOT closed under intersection.

solution.result: FALSE — the intersection of two CFLs is not necessarily a CFL. Counterexample: {a^n b^n c^m} ∩ {a^m b^n c^n} = {a^n b^n c^n}, which is not a CFL.

questions.formula_note: CFL Pumping Lemma used to prove {a^n b^n c^n} is not context-free; standard counterexample for non-closure of CFLs under intersection.

questions.question:
Context-free languages are: (Select all that apply)

A. closed under union
B. closed under complementation
C. closed under intersection
D. closed under Kleene closure
questions.to_find: Which closure properties hold for the class of context-free languages

understand.plain: Context-free languages (CFLs) are closed under some operations but not others. We need to determine which of union, complementation, intersection, and Kleene star preserve the context-free property. CFLs are closed under union and Kleene closure but NOT under complementation or intersection — these are fundamental facts in the theory of computation.

keywords.explain: If L1 and L2 are CFLs, then L1 union L2 is also a CFL.
keywords.example: L1 = {a^n b^n}, L2 = {a^n b^m c^m}: their union is context-free (combine their grammars with S -> S1 | S2).

keywords.explain: L* = {epsilon} union L union LL union LLL union ... (all finite concatenations of strings from L).
keywords.example: If L = {a^n b^n | n >= 1}, then L* is also context-free.

keywords.explain: The complement of a CFL is not necessarily a CFL — CFLs are NOT closed under complementation.
keywords.example: The complement of {a^n b^n c^n}^c would involve non-CFL structure; more directly, if CFLs were closed under complement, they'd be closed under intersection (De Morgan), contradiction.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 500 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='480' height='210' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>CFL Closure Properties</text><rect x='30' y='55' width='200' height='40' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='130' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>Union: CLOSED (YES)</text><rect x='260' y='55' width='200' height='40' rx='6' class='no'/><text x='360' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>Complementation: NOT closed</text><rect x='30' y='110' width='200' height='40' rx='6' class='no'/><text x='130' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>Intersection: NOT closed</text><rect x='260' y='110' width='200' height='40' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='360' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>Kleene closure: CLOSED (YES)</text><rect x='100' y='165' width='300' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='250' y='183' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Correct answers: A (union) and D (Kleene star)</text><text x='250' y='200' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Note: Closed under concatenation too, but not listed</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing CFL closure properties: closed under union (yes) and Kleene closure (yes), but not closed under complementation (no) and intersection (no).

given.aim: Determine which of union, complementation, intersection, and Kleene closure preserve the context-free property

terms.meaning: If L1 and L2 are CFLs with grammars G1 (start S1) and G2 (start S2), introduce new start S -> S1 | S2
terms.example: G: S -> S1 | S2, where S1 and S2 are starts of the original grammars — the combined grammar is still a CFG
terms.connects: This construction always works, proving CFLs are closed under union — Option A is TRUE.

terms.meaning: The complement of a CFL need not be a CFL — this closure does not hold
terms.example: If CFLs were closed under complement, then L1 ∩ L2 = complement(complement(L1) union complement(L2)) would also be CFL (by De Morgan), contradicting non-closure under intersection
terms.connects: Option B is FALSE — CFLs are not closed under complementation.

terms.meaning: The intersection of two CFLs need not be a CFL
terms.example: {a^n b^n c^m} ∩ {a^m b^n c^n} = {a^n b^n c^n} which is not a CFL
terms.connects: Option C is FALSE — CFLs are not closed under intersection.

terms.term: CFL closed under Kleene closure L* (Option D)
terms.meaning: If L is a CFL with grammar G (start S), then L* is generated by adding S -> SS | epsilon to G
terms.example: For L = {a^n b^n | n >= 1}, L* is CFL with S -> SS | epsilon | aAb, A -> aAb | ab — still context-free
terms.connects: Option D is TRUE — CFLs are closed under Kleene star.

given.plan: Check each closure property: union (closed — combine grammars), complementation (not closed — De Morgan argument using non-closure under intersection), intersection (not closed — counterexample with {a^n b^n c^n}), Kleene star (closed — add S -> SS | epsilon).

steps.title: Verify closure under union (Option A)
steps.formula_raw: G: S -> S1 | S2 (new start symbol with productions to both original starts)
steps.apply: Given CFGs G1 with start S1 and G2 with start S2, create G with fresh start symbol S and add production S -> S1 | S2 (along with all rules from G1 and G2). Any string derivable from S1 or S2 is derivable from S. The result is a valid CFG, so L(G) = L(G1) union L(G2) is a CFL. CFLs are CLOSED under union.
steps.note: This construction works for any two CFGs and is a standard proof.

steps.title: Verify non-closure under complementation (Option B) and intersection (Option C)
steps.apply: Counterexample for intersection: L1 = {a^n b^n c^m | n,m >= 1} (CFL) and L2 = {a^m b^n c^n | n,m >= 1} (CFL), but L1 ∩ L2 = {a^n b^n c^n | n >= 1} which is NOT a CFL (shown by pumping lemma). For complementation: if CFLs were closed under complement, then by De Morgan L1 ∩ L2 = complement(complement(L1) union complement(L2)) would be CFL (since CFLs are closed under union), contradicting the above. So CFLs are NOT closed under complementation either.
steps.note: Non-closure under intersection and complementation are linked by De Morgan's law.

steps.title: Verify closure under Kleene star (Option D)
steps.formula_raw: G*: S_new -> S_new S | epsilon (where S is the start of original grammar G for L)
steps.apply: Given a CFL L with CFG G (start symbol S), construct G* with new start S_new and add rules: S_new -> S_new S | epsilon. Then L(G*) = L* since S_new can generate zero or more copies of strings from L. This is a valid CFG, so L* is a CFL. CFLs are CLOSED under Kleene star.
steps.note: CFLs are also closed under concatenation: add S_new -> S1 S2 to combine two grammars.

solution.result: Options A (union) and D (Kleene closure) are correct. CFLs are closed under union and Kleene star, but NOT under complementation or intersection.

questions.formula_note: CFL closure under union proved by grammar union construction (S -> S1 | S2). Kleene closure proved by S* -> S* S | epsilon. Non-closure under intersection proved by the {a^n b^n c^n} counterexample and De Morgan's law links it to non-closure under complementation.
